Title: NST duo join 2026 Khazanah Wolfson programme at Cambridge
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: New Straits Times specialist writer Iylia Marsya Iskandar and Business Times editor Muhammed Ahmad Hamdan have been selected for the 2026 Khazanah Wolfson Press Fellowship.
The other recipients are James Wong, business reporter at The Borneo Post, and Zufazlin Baharuddin, journalist with Bernama.
All four will undertake a 10-week fellowship under the Khazanah Residency Programme, organised by Khazanah Nasional Berhad at Wolfson College, University of Cambridge in the United Kingdom, from May 4 to July 10.
This is the first time four journalists have been selected in a single group, reflecting a balance between emerging and experienced media professionals.
Khazanah managing director Datuk Amirul Feisal Wan Zahir said journalists today play a critical role in navigating a complex information landscape.
"In today's information environment, journalists are frontline defenders against misinformation. This raises the bar for journalism.
"We congratulate the 2026 cohort and hope the experience will strengthen their work in promoting a more informed public discourse," he said at the letter of award ceremony today.
Media Prima News and Current Affairs deputy group managing editor and New Straits Times group editor Farrah Naz Abd Karim also attended the ceremony.
She said the selection of two NST journalists was recognition of both individual talent and newsroom standards.
"It's a recognition not just of their individual talent, but of the standards we're trying to uphold in our newsroom.
"This fellowship is also an opportunity for Malaysian journalism, exposing practitioners to global best practices that they can bring back and adapt locally.
"Investment like this is important at a time when trust in the media is being tested everywhere.
"We see this as part of a longer journey to strengthen credible and responsible journalism in Malaysia," she said.
Muhammed said he was surprised and grateful to be selected.
"I did not expect to be selected, so I was really surprised and very grateful," he said.
He said the fellowship would provide valuable exposure to global newsroom practices.
"With the journalism industry evolving rapidly, particularly with the rise of social media, it is important for us to gain insights from global perspectives on how newsrooms operate," he said.
He added that he hoped to better balance speed and depth in reporting.
"I want to explore how we can strike that balance while improving newsroom operations and the quality of our reporting," he said.
Iylia said the fellowship was a rare opportunity to step back from newsroom pressures and reflect on her work.
"My research will look at how Malaysian journalists can improve reporting on refugees and build trust with vulnerable communities.
"I hope to give a voice to those often unheard and promote more dignified and ethical reporting," she said, adding she was grateful for the support of editors Farrah Naz and Sharanjit Singh who nominated her.
Wong said he hoped to bring a younger perspective to the batch.
"I am very honoured and thankful for this opportunity. As one of the youngest in the cohort, I hope to bring a fresher perspective that younger audiences can relate to.
"My research focuses on how journalism can help sustain indigenous languages, including learning from the UK's experience in reviving the Welsh language and applying it in the Malaysian context, particularly in Sarawak," he said.
Zufazlin said her research would examine how media shapes awareness of emergency funds and financial preparedness.
"I want to understand how people consume and process news, and how it influences their financial decisions, especially in times of global uncertainty.
"This is an opportunity to strengthen journalism's role in promoting financial literacy," she said.
Since its inception in 2013, the programme has supported 33 Malaysians, including journalists and corporate communications professionals, in enhancing reporting standards and industry practices.
The fellowship allows participants to pursue independent research, attend seminars and engage with international media and academic communities, and contribute to the journalism ecosystem upon their return.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Another four years before flood situation in Selangor improves, says exco
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Long-term flood mitigation efforts are still underway in Selangor and it will take at least four years before the results can be seen, the state government said.
Selangor Infrastructure and Agriculture committee chairman Datuk Izham Hashim said the state acknowledged concerns raised by Sultan of Selangor, Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah, over the persistent flood issues, but added that works are ongoing to address the matter.
"Flood problems in Selangor are unlikely to be fully resolved anytime soon, as major mitigation projects remain ongoing," Izham told reporters after the state legislative assembly meeting here today (Tuesday).
He said the state would need at least another four years before the measures, currently being implemented in phases, are completed.
This comes a day after the Selangor ruler expressed his disappointment that his repeated calls for stronger flood mitigation measures in the state remain unheeded.
Speaking at the opening of the Fourth Session of the 15th Selangor State Legislative Assembly on Monday, Sultan Sharafuddin had said his earlier advice, including a suggestion to seek expertise from the Netherlands, appeared to have been ignored. He advised the state government to prioritise addressing the flood problem in the state.
Izham said flood mitigation projects are not something that can be completed in a short period, such as a month or even a year.
"From planning and design to implementation, it takes a minimum of three to four years, and must comply with all technical and regulatory procedures," he said.
"Key works, including river widening and deepening, are actively being carried out but require time before their effectiveness can be fully felt on the ground."
He added that Selangor has adopted various modern water management approaches from countries such as the Netherlands, China and Germany, but cautioned that their effectiveness differs due to local conditions.
"Although Selangor has taken reference from various international water management models, including nature-based solutions and smart city systems, the challenge here is far more complex due to significantly higher annual rainfall.
"In the Netherlands, annual rainfall is around 700 to 900 millimetres, whereas Selangor receives nearly 3,000 millimetres, about four times higher. This is a major difference," he said.
He said such conditions place greater demands on infrastructure, particularly in terms of drainage capacity, retention ponds and pump systems in low-lying areas.
"Even though we adopt foreign technologies, we have to be realistic. Our soil structure, development density and rainfall levels are very different. We cannot blindly adopt these systems without adapting them to local conditions," Izham said.
He said the state is also exploring multiple approaches, including smart city technologies from China and systems implemented in Germany's flood-prone regions, in preparing Selangor's water master plan.
"What's most important is not just adopting technology, but how we adapt it to suit the real conditions in Selangor," he said.
He said the state government respects all views but remains focused on implementing its Water Master Plan and Flood Mitigation Master Plan, which are being rolled out in stages.
[21/4/2026 5:23 PM] Lionel M (NST): Izham also said that the total value of flood mitigation projects in Selangor currently stands at about RM4 billion, with works ongoing across multiple locations.
"The public can monitor the progress of these projects transparently through the Department of Irrigation and Drainage (JPS) portal," he said.
On flash floods, Izham said the most practical solution at present is the construction of retention ponds, including underground systems where land constraints exist.
"Flash floods are a different phenomenon. Intense rainfall over a short period overwhelms drains and rivers. So, we need to temporarily store the water in retention ponds before pumping it out," he said.
Selangor currently has more than 600 retention ponds, including former mining pools, with 185 of them having been audited to improve water management efficiency.
A heavy downpour on Monday evening triggered flash floods in several parts of the Klang Valley. Among the areas affected were Bukit Raja in Klang, Section 13 in Shah Alam, Kampung Melayu Subang and Jalan KTM Sungai Buloh.
In Kuala Lumpur, flash floods were reported in Jalan Duta, Jalan Ampang and Jalan Nagasari.
Kuala Lumpur Mayor Datuk Seri Fadlun Mak Ujud visited several of these flood-hit locations yesterday, including Bukit Lanjan, where a landslide also occurred.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police seize RM4.6mil worth of vape devices and drugs in Op Vape crackdown
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have seized 8,091 vape devices, 5,257 cartridges and 19.67kg of drugs, including 18.55kg of liquid believed to contain furanyl fentanyl, in a nationwide crackdown under Op Vape 1.0 conducted from April 13 to 17.
Bukit Aman Narcotics Criminal Investigation Department (NCID) director Datuk Hussein Omar Khan said 20 people were arrested for various drug-related offences following inspections of 1,670 vape premises nationwide.
"Of those detained, 13 were arrested under Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act, five under Section 39B for drug trafficking, one under Section 12(2), and another under the Poisons Act," he told reporters here on Tuesday.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-official describes pressure from UK PM's office, deepening Mandelson row
Article:
LONDON: A former top foreign ministry official said on Tuesday he had faced "constant pressure" from Prime Minister Keir Starmer's office to speed up the process to install his pick as US ambassador, deepening a row that threatens the British leader.
A war of words over who should ultimately take the blame for appointing Labour veteran Peter Mandelson to Britain's highest diplomatic post despite his past history and known ties to late US sex offender Jeffrey Epstein has piled pressure on Starmer, prompting calls by critics for him to quit.
Starmer has said he was "wrong" to appoint Mandelson to the role and has expressed regret, but on Monday put the blame firmly on officials for failing to tell him that a security vetting body had advised against his appointment - something, he added, would have stopped him from employing the new ambassador.
On Tuesday, it was the turn of Olly Robbins, who was sacked late on Thursday after Starmer and foreign minister Yvette Cooper said they had lost confidence in him, to mount his defence.
He was quick to turn the focus on Starmer's office.
"I walked into a situation in which there was already a very, very strong expectation ... that he needed to be in post and in America as quickly as humanly possible," Robbins told a parliamentary committee.
"I think throughout January (2025), honestly, my office, the foreign secretary's office, were under constant pressure," Robbins said.
"There was an atmosphere of constant chasing," he said, describing "very frequent phone calls" from the private office of Starmer's Number 10 Downing Street.
He said the vetting body had advised the appointment was a borderline case and they were leaning against granting clearance - a message Starmer says his government never received.
Robbins' words are likely to increase the pressure on Starmer, who after winning the largest majority in modern history for Labour at a national election in 2024 is facing new calls to step down over a scandal which has run for months.
Labour lawmakers have said there would not be an immediate move to oust Starmer over the scandal, especially as the party is expected to suffer big losses in local elections in England and regional votes in Wales and Scotland in just over two weeks.
But the return of the focus on Starmer's Downing Street operation will do little to appease those lawmakers who have repeatedly raised concerns over its operation, criticising what some call a bunker mentality and lack of access.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RTD, Media Prima to host mega carnival with free helmets, discounts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Road Transport Department (RTD) is partnering with Media Prima Bhd for the JPJ Madani Mega Carnival and Karnival Jom Heboh in conjunction with its 80th anniversary.
The carnival, which runs from April 24 to 26 at the Batu Kawan State Stadium in Penang, offers various activities for visitors.
RTD director-general Datuk Aedy Fadly Ramli said highlights include a free helmet exchange programme for those who bring their old headgear.
"A total of 5,000 helmets will be given away for free. We will also have advocacy programmes, electric vehicle (EV) exhibitions, and a 50 per cent discount on vehicle registration numbers.
"Nearly 50,000 registration numbers will be offered. Many other interesting activities have been lined up throughout the three-day programme," he said when met during the Malaysia Hari Ini (MHI) programme at Balai Berita today.
Aedy Fadly added that the department is continuously boosting advocacy and enforcement activities to reduce the national road accident rate.
He said RTD is also leveraging the latest technology in its enforcement efforts, including the use of drones and Automated Number Plate Recognition (ANPR).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mass shooting reignites push to loosen gun laws in war-torn Ukraine
Article:
A deadly shooting in Ukraine's capital at the weekend has fuelled calls for the loosening of restrictions on the right to own and carry handguns for self-defence in a country scarred by years of war against Russia.
Hours after a gunman took hostages in a Kyiv supermarket on Saturday, shooting seven people dead, many Ukrainians turned to social media to demand the right to bear handguns for their own protection.
Many others condemned the idea, in alarm.
"If the people who encountered the terrorist today had been armed, there wouldn't have been so many victims," Deputy Commander of the 3rd Army Corps, Maksym Zhorin, said on the Telegram app.
"Legalising handguns is the only correct conclusion to draw from this tragic event."
Civilians in Ukraine are not allowed to carry firearms, and there is no comprehensive law regulating armed self-defence.
But in the wake of Russia's invasion in 2022, civilians lined up as the military and police handed out weapons to repel the invaders, on the condition that they should be returned when the war ended.
Ihor Fris, co-author of a draft law on civilian firearm circulation that passed its first reading in 2022, argues that while criminals will find ways to get weapons, civilians in Ukraine are left with no means of defending themselves.
Fris, from the ruling Servant of the People party, told Reuters that lawmakers, the interior ministry, and experts will discuss preparing the draft for a second reading in the near future.
"In my view, today we could allow Ukrainians to own short-barrelled firearms – weapons for self-defence at home," he said, adding that the law should come into force in a year once passed.
Under pressure over the police's mishandling of the shooting, Interior Minister Ihor Klymenko has voiced his support for the measure: "I believe that people should have the right to armed self-defence," Klymenko said.
While historically most Ukrainians have not backed firearms liberalisation, attitudes appear to have shifted since the war.
The largest poll to date was run by the state in mid-2022 and showed that 59 per cent of Ukrainians supported the right to carry a handgun in public places, while 22 per cent categorically rejected the idea.
Over 1.7 million people took part in the survey, with 19 per cent saying they are for gun rights but are against carrying them in public.
"I'm actually very much against the call for firearms for people," said Daryna, a 31-year-old resident of the Holosiivskyi district, where the shooting took place.
"Because we see what's happening in the US Firearms are allowed there and there are many more such situations there."
In the latest mass shooting event in the US, a gunman killed seven of his children and an eighth minor in a domestic violence incident on Sunday in Louisiana, before police shot him dead during a vehicle chase.
Unlike in the US, shooting rampages have remained extremely rare in Ukraine.
In 2021, according to the interior ministry, 273 cases of weapons-related violence were registered, including incidents and accidents with explosives.
By 2023, the figure had risen to over 11,000.
Some of those opposed to gun liberalisation say it is unclear how the right to carry guns will prevent more victims during mass shootings.
[[nid:1423124]]
"I see many arguments in favour from people who have never been in a situation where they had to decide whether to use one, let alone actually use one and understand the consequences," Roman Kostenko, a member of the parliamentary defence and security committee from the Holos party, said.
According to Kostenko, who has extensive battlefield experience, there is a lack of understanding of how ill-suited Ukraine's legal system is to ruling on cases of armed self-defence.
Many opponents say expanding access to firearms would make them more easily available to those intending to do harm.
The Kyiv shooter had a registered weapon.
"The system has given a weapon to this man. Why do we assume that, in the future, only good people will be given arms to protect us from the bad ones?" Inna Sovsun, another lawmaker from Holos, said.
Fris argues that a tightly regulated system of checks for gun permit applicants and gun schools, akin to licensed driving academies, would minimise such risks.
A transitional period of at least one year would be required to build such a system, he said, adding that it is even more critical to amend Ukraine's criminal code to define the rights and limits of self-defence.
The consequences of allowing handguns to circulate widely in a society with an extremely high level of war-related trauma, including post-traumatic stress disorder, are a major concern voiced by many people.
But supporters argue that guns are already in wide circulation, following the state's distribution of weapons in the early days of war and millions used in the fight against Russia.
Ukrainians may hold up to seven million undeclared small arms, Heorhiy Uchaykin, who chairs the Ukrainian Gun Owners' Association, told Reuters.
Ukraine's interior ministry did not respond to Reuters' request for statistics on gun ownership.
"If the constitution guarantees me the right to defend my own life, I must have the means to do so," said Uchaykin, who supports the right to carry handguns and has been lobbying for it for years.
He criticised local police for failing to protect residents in Saturday's shooting. Several officers were filmed running away from the scene, prompting the resignation of a police chief and criminal cases.
Many lawmakers, however, are cautious about any liberalisation of gun laws and urge against any rapid changes.
"We must not legalise guns based on emotions after tragedies," said Olha Vasylevska-Smahliuk, a lawmaker from the ruling party.
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perak Mufti Dept warns on 'office wife, office husband' trend in workplace relationships
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Perak Mufti Department has issued a warning against the growing "office wife" and "office husband" phenomenon, saying overly close relationships between colleagues of the opposite sex may lead to emotional impropriety and affect workplace harmony and family institutions.
In the statement, titled "Adab Pergaulan Semasa Bekerja: Menangani Fenomena Office Wife-Husband Menurut Perspektif Syariah" and published via its official Facebook page, the department said that Islam permitted women to work and earn a lawful livelihood, citing Sayyidatina Khadijah as an example of a respected businesswoman during the time of Prophet Muhammad, provided proper ethical boundaries were observed.
However, it stressed that workplace interactions must remain within Islamic guidelines, particularly in relation to ikhtilat (mixing of unrelated men and women) and khalwat (seclusion).
The statement said that the terms "office wife" and "office husband" generally referred to close relationships between male and female colleagues who may share personal matters, emotional concerns and daily routines in a manner resembling that of spouses.
While such relationships might begin innocently as professional camaraderie, it warned that they could gradually develop into emotional dependency and inappropriate attachment, potentially affecting marital relationships and social stability.
The department said that maintaining proper boundaries at the workplace was intended to preserve dignity, protect family institutions and uphold harmony within society.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mara targets June law change for stronger governance
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A draft amendment to the Majlis Amanah Rakyat (Mara) Act 1996 is being targeted for tabling in Parliament as early as June, in a move aimed at ensuring the agency's legal framework remains relevant to modern governance needs.
Mara chairman Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said the amendment is also intended to align the organisation's management with current developments and future challenges, particularly in the areas of integrity and transparency.
He said the existing law did not adequately address the separation of powers within the organisation or broader institutional governance.
Asyraf said the draft amendment had been completed and was now in the engagement phase with various stakeholders, including the Finance Ministry, Economy Ministry and parties involved in legal affairs.
"Mara is also conducting a series of engagement sessions nationwide to obtain broader views from stakeholders.
"This process is important to ensure the amendments are truly comprehensive and take into account various perspectives before they are submitted for Cabinet approval.
"On Mara's part, we are aiming to table it during the June Parliament sitting. However, final approval for the amendment is subject to Cabinet consideration before it is brought to Parliament for debate and passage," he said at Mara's annual media briefing here today.
Asyraf said there was a need to amend the law, as the management of an organisation should no longer be anchored to an ordinance or Act rooted in the 1990s.
"There was a time in Parliament when questions were raised over how the salary or allowance of a board member in an organisation could reach tens of thousands. Upon scrutiny, it was found that a person could sit on seven or eight committees, all approved by the board of directors.
"Legally, there may be no issue. But morally, and from the standpoint of governance, how can someone serving on an investment committee also sit on the board of directors? That is a conflict of interest. But such matters were never discussed back in the 1970s," he said.
He added the amendment was not merely a technical change, but aimed at strengthening governance principles so the organisation would remain consistent even when leadership changes.
"Leaders may come and go, but the system and governance must remain intact. That is why these aspects must be embedded in the Act, so that they are binding and serve as a continuous guide," he said.
He also cited cases involving the purchase of assets abroad at above-market prices, which resulted in losses.
"This stems from weaknesses in governance. Matters like this must be corrected so that Mara can move forward with greater integrity and earn public confidence," he said, adding that the proposed establishment of a syariah advisory committee was among the key components of the amendment.
"This is because Mara manages public funds, especially those involving Bumiputera, who expect every aspect of its operations to comply with syariah principles. It is not only about financial instruments, but also the organisation's overall operations and management," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Drug ring using money changers as front busted, 19 arrested
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have dismantled a drug trafficking syndicate that lived in luxury while allegedly using money-changing businesses as a front to launder illicit proceeds, following the arrest of 19 people in a series of raids across the Klang Valley on April 16.
Bukit Aman Narcotics Crime Investigation Department (NCID) director Datuk Hussein Omar Khan said those detained comprised 12 local men and seven women, including five Malaysians and two Chinese nationals, aged between 24 and 48.
He said authorities also seized cash in multiple currencies amounting to an estimated RM4.9 million, believed to be proceeds from drug trafficking.
"The first raid was conducted at an apartment in Taman Wahyu here, leading to the arrest of two suspects. Various types of drugs were seized, including 0.89kg of ecstasy pills, 0.69kg of ketamine, 0.46kg of Erimin 5 and 6.5kg of MDMA powder, as well as equipment used to process drugs," he told a press conference at the Kuala Lumpur police contingent headquarters today.
Hussein said follow-up raids in Kuala Lumpur and Kepong led to further arrests.
"In another raid at an apartment in Kepong, we seized 31kg of ketamine and 3.8kg of Erimin 5.
"We believe the syndicate has been active since early this year, using apartments as storage and processing centres before distributing drugs across the Klang Valley," he said.
Investigations revealed that the 12th suspect was the syndicate leader, while the 10th suspect acted as his assistant. Others were believed to be storekeepers and runners.
Hussein said total drug seizures amounted to 31.8kg of ketamine, 4.2kg of Erimin 5, 0.89kg of ecstasy pills and 6.5kg of MDMA, with an estimated street value of RM3.1 million.
Police also seized 18 luxury vehicles, including a Rolls-Royce Cullinan Black Badge, Porsche Taycan 4S and several Mercedes-Benz AMG models.
"The total value of seizures and confiscations is estimated at RM14.15 million. The drugs seized are believed to be capable of supplying about 151,000 users," he said.
Initial urine screenings found three suspects tested positive for drugs, while background checks showed five had prior criminal records.
"Four suspects have been remanded for seven days from April 17 to 23, while seven others were detained under the Dangerous Drugs (Special Preventive Measures) Act 1985.
"Eight others were released on police bail. The case is being investigated under Section 39B of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, which carries the mandatory death penalty or life imprisonment, and not less than 12 strokes of the cane upon conviction," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Welfare Dept seeks fit next of kin for boy allegedly abused by aunt, uncle
Article:
KUALA KRAI: A 10-year-old boy who was allegedly abused by his aunt and uncle is in stable condition but remains traumatised.
Kelantan Welfare Department director Che Samsuzuki Che Noh said the child is currently under close monitoring by doctors at Kuala Krai Hospital.
"I have visited him today and found that he is physically healthy and appears cheerful, but he is still traumatised from the incident," he said when met at the hospital.
Che Samsuzuki added that counsellors from the department would conduct activities to help the child cope with the trauma.
He said the department has also held discussions with the hospital's authorities on the next course of action for the boy.
"We will ensure the child is protected in a safe place while we try to locate and contact suitable next of kin who are fit to care for him," he said, adding that the department is expected to transfer the boy from hospital within the week.
Che Samsuzuki said this was the first such case recorded in the area in the past three years.
The case is being investigated under Section 19(2)(a) of the Child Act.
A 10-year-old boy was recently hospitalised after he was a
llegedly abused by his aunt and uncle
at their rented home in a village in Machang.
Sources said the child suffered bruises all over his body after he was allegedly beaten with a cane and clothes hanger as punishment for misbehaviour.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DID records one river in KL exceeding danger level
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Department of Irrigation and Drainage (JPS) has recorded one of 29 rivers around the capital exceeding the danger level this afternoon.
According to its website, the situation involves the water level at the outlet station of the Flat Sri Johor Retention Pond (F2), despite no rainfall being recorded.
The water level at the station reached 33.03m at 2.30pm today, exceeding the 32.66m danger threshold by 0.37m with an upward trend.
The normal threshold for the station is 27.5m, followed by the alert level at 31.66m and the warning level at 32.16m.
In other states, two stations recorded levels exceeding the warning stage: Sungai Bidor at Changkat Jong in Hilir Perak and Sungai Perlis at Bukit Tok Poh (F2) in Kangar.
At Sungai Bidor, the water level was recorded at 3.49m, which is 0.27m above the warning level.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Modern education needs the wisdom of civilisational history
Article:
LETTERS:
In an age where artificial intelligence can generate answers in seconds, a more fundamental question emerges - what does it truly mean to seek knowledge?
For centuries, civilisations have answered this question in different ways.
Yet, when we look at two historically rich regions — the Iranian world and the Nusantara (the Malay-Indonesian archipelago) — we find not a contradiction, but a powerful complementarity.
Iran's civilisational experience demonstrates one enduring strength: a deep intellectual tradition grounded in rigor, system, and scientific inquiry.
From the ancient Academy of Gundishapur to towering figures such as Ibn Sina and Al-Biruni, knowledge has been pursued with seriousness and discipline.
It was institutionalized, debated, and expanded — forming a strong foundation for scientific and philosophical advancement.
The Nusantara, on the other hand, offers a different but equally vital strength: the ability to embed knowledge within culture, ethics, and everyday life.
When Islam arrived in the region, it did not displace local traditions; it engaged them. Religious teachings were translated into lived values — into social harmony, mutual care, and moral responsibility.
In Indonesian 'pesantren', for instance, students do not simply study texts — they learn humility, discipline, and social responsibility. Knowledge is not merely something to possess, but something to embody.
These two traditions, when seen together, reveal a crucial insight. Iran reminds us that knowledge must be deep, structured, and intellectually rigorous.
The Nusantara reminds us that knowledge must be ethical, lived, and socially meaningful.
Today, modern education often separates these dimensions. Knowledge is reduced to information. Learning becomes transactional. Success is measured by certificates rather than character.
This reduction is not only misleading — it is dangerous.
The global challenges we face today — from technological disruption to social fragmentation — cannot be solved by technical expertise alone.
They require a more holistic understanding of knowledge: one that integrates reason and ethics, science and wisdom, global insight and local grounding.
This is where an inter-civilisational perspective becomes essential.
By learning from the intellectual rigor of the Iranian tradition and the ethical-cultural grounding of the Nusantara, we can begin to reimagine education — not merely as a system of instruction, but as a process of human formation.
Across Southeast Asia and beyond, concerns are growing about the loss of 'moral direction' in education.
At the same time, traditional systems are often seen as inadequate for modern demands.
But perhaps the real solution lies not in choosing between tradition and modernity, but in bridging them.
Civilisations do not advance by abandoning their past, but by reinterpreting it.
Iran and the Nusantara show us that knowledge has always been more than accumulation — it is about transformation.
As we move deeper into the digital age, the real challenge may not be how much we know, but how we understand, live, and use what we know.
Learning across civilisations may be exactly what we need.
MEHDI SOLTANZADEH
Academy of Malay Studies
Universiti Malaya;
ARIES MUSNANDAR
Universitas Islam Raden Rahmat
Indonesia
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ministry ramps up enforcement to curb inflation driven by global disruptions
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government is stepping up measures to control the supply and prices of goods to shield the local market from inflationary pressures arising from the global supply crisis.
Deputy Minister of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living (KPDN) Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh said Malaysia is not insulated from global impacts, including conflicts in Middle East and restrictions on key trade routes such as the Strait of Hormuz.
She said rising international logistics costs are contributing to higher import prices, affecting the entire domestic supply chain.
"Actually, we do not live in our own cocoon because today, whatever happens around us will have an impact on our country. When logistics costs increase, surely the prices of goods brought in will rise, and there will be costs throughout the supply chain," she said.
She made the remarks as a guest on Bernama Radio's exclusive interview programme titled "From Global to Local: Government Effort to Control Inflation" today.
Fuziah also said that to minimise the impact on the people, the KPDN is focusing on ensuring sufficient supply and controlled prices through enforcement of laws such as the Control of Supplies Act 1961 and the Price Control and Anti‑Profiteering Act 2011 (Act 723).
She added that enforcement operations, such as Ops Tiris, have been intensified to prevent leakages of subsidised goods, including diesel, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) and packet cooking oil, which are at higher risk during global crises.
"Besides Ops Tiris, the government also uses these two Acts to control the supply and prices of goods so that sellers or traders do not arbitrarily use the current conflict and geopolitical situation in Middle East as reasons to increase the prices of goods," she said.
She said the KPDN has the authority to instruct traders to submit complete information on business costs for up to two years to identify any unjustified price increases.
She also suggested that consumers prioritise local goods and practise prudent spending to help stabilise the market amid current global uncertainties.– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Geopolitical tensions open window for Penang to court global business events
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Penang is seeing a surge in opportunities to host international conferences as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East force organisers to relocate events.
Penang Convention and Exhibition Bureau (PCEB) chief executive officer Ashwin Gunasekeran said the shifting landscape has led to a redistribution of major business events, with Asia-Pacific emerging as a key alternative region.
"We are seeing a significant increase in opportunities as conferences originally planned for the Middle Eastern region are being cancelled or moved.
"Thousands of events are now being redistributed across Asia-Pacific and Europe," he said after the launch of the BE Associate programme by PCEB here today.
Ashwin said Penang has identified 26 international conferences as immediate targets for this year and next.
"These are what we consider low-hanging fruits. The conferences are already established, and we only need to present Penang as a viable and compelling host destination," he said.
He said that, so far, the impact on Penang has been minimal, with no outright cancellations recorded.
"There have only been a few postponements, typically by one to two months, largely due to travel-related concerns.
"Overall, the situation remains manageable," he said.
Ashwin said competition among destinations in the Asia-Pacific region is expected to intensify as countries move quickly to secure displaced events.
"This is a window of opportunity, but also a highly competitive space. Many destinations are going after the same pool of conferences," he said.
Meanwhile, Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said the state would continue leveraging its business events sector to support tourism growth.
He said the state government would work closely with industry players to strengthen Penang's value proposition and position it as a preferred destination for global business events.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Illicit cigarettes make up 60pct of Malaysia market, report says
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Illicit cigarettes make up about 60 per cent of Malaysia's market, with six in 10 packs untaxed as syndicates tighten their grip.
According to Nielsen, cigarettes are smuggled through unguarded entry points from Vietnam, China and Indonesia, using containers up to 10m long that can carry up to 10 million sticks.
If sold in Malaysia, syndicates can make profits of up to RM2 million per container.
Heavy smokers in the country are estimated at more than four million, consuming an average of 30 cigarettes a day.
Studies show smuggling syndicates generate about RM5 billion annually from the illicit cigarette trade, while the government loses billions of ringgit in unpaid taxes.
The report stated that although the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has taken action, there are still weaknesses in enforcement.
According to the report, illicit cigarettes can enter the country due to several factors, including gaps in border control between Vietnam and Indonesia that have yet to be fully addressed.
It said enforcement at sea between Terengganu and Johor remains weak, allowing illegal cigarettes to enter undetected.
Latest data shows that 90 per cent of smuggled cigarettes enter Malaysia through coastal areas, using speedboats and ship-to-ship (STS) operations.
Meanwhile, a previous study by the Institute for Democracy and Economic Affairs (IDEAS) also highlighted weaknesses in enforcement and coordination among agencies as contributing factors to the smuggling problem.
It said shortcomings in import procedures have encouraged syndicates to continue operating.
The study suggested strengthening enforcement by tightening monitoring at sea and on land, and enhancing cooperation and information-sharing between agencies to effectively combat syndicates.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indonesian expert warns of cross-border security threats in region
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Human trafficking, drug trafficking and terrorism pose major cross-border threats in the region, an Indonesian security expert has warned.
Dino Hindarto, president of the Indonesian chapter of the Asia Pacific Security Association (APSA), said such threats often spill over beyond national borders.
"If an incident occurs or risks escalate in one country, neighbouring countries will also be affected," he said.
He was speaking on the sidelines of a plenary session on "Securing Borders, Safeguarding Cities: Collaborative Strategies for Regional Security" at the Defence Ministry's pavilion during Defence Services Asia (DSA) and National Security Asia (NATSEC) 2026.
Hindarto also said regular dialogue and stronger regional coordination are needed to enhance border security and tackle transnational crime.
Drawing on past regional experience, including terrorism incidents, Hindarto said cooperation through intelligence-sharing, joint training and coordinated exercises has proven effective in mitigating threats.
He added that, beyond intelligence-sharing, joint exercises are important in strengthening preparedness and response among enforcement agencies.
"Training and exercises are important. It is not just about sharing information, but ensuring all parties are ready to act together," he said.
Echoing similar sentiments, Port Klang Malaysia Border Protection and Control Agency commander Datuk Nik Ezanee Mohd Faisal said coordination among enforcement agencies should not follow a rigid structure but should be tailored to the urgency and nature of the threat.
Communication and engagement between agencies must be flexible, with the frequency of coordination determined on a case-by-case basis, he said.
"For counterterrorism, we are consistently exchanging intelligence on extremism, including individuals linked to Daesh or Islamic State, their travel patterns and communications via platforms such as WhatsApp, Telegram, Instagram and TikTok.
"If we miss that communication, we risk overlooking a threat that could escalate into something serious," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kundasang earthquake causes no damage or injuries: Fire Department
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: A weak earthquake struck Kundasang, Ranau at 3.02am today, but no structural damage or injuries have been reported.
The Sabah Fire and Rescue Department (JBPM) said in a statement that a monitoring operation, which began at 6.35am, involved inspections of shop premises, government buildings and public areas around Ranau town.
"Based on information from the public and observations by building caretakers, no structural damage has been reported so far and the situation remained under control and safe. No injuries or subsequent incidents have been reported," the statement said.
However, the department said it would continue monitoring the situation from time to time and remained prepared for any possible aftershocks.
The public were also urged to immediately report any damage to the authorities and to adhere to official instructions from government agencies.
Earlier, the Malaysian Meteorological Department via its official website reported that the magnitude 3.4 earthquake occurred at a depth of 10 kilometres, centred at coordinates of 6.1 degrees north and 116.7 degrees east, about eight kilometres northwest of Ranau.– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Geologists to probe Cameron Highlands flash floods
Article:
PETALING JAYA: The Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability Ministry will deploy geologists to Cameron Highlands to study the recent flash floods and provide technical assistance.
Its minister, Datuk Seri Arthur Joseph Kurup, said the ministry would work with several agencies, including the Public Works Department (JKR), the Department of Irrigation and Drainage (JPS) and local authorities, to identify the cause of the incident and measures to prevent a recurrence.
"We are aware of the incident, which occurred last night. This effort requires cooperation between multiple ministries and agencies.
"For our part, we will send geologists to conduct studies and provide technical assistance. A report will be issued once investigations are completed to determine what happened and how we can avoid similar incidents in the future," he told reporters after attending the Climate Change and Sustainability Conference 2026.
Heavy rain lasting more than two hours
on Sunday triggered flash floods in the Habu Heights area in Cameron Highlands.
Cameron Highlands police chief Azri Ramli said the downpour, which began at 2pm, caused muddy water from surrounding hills to surge onto the Ringlet main road.
No casualties were reported, although two police reports were lodged involving vehicles swept by strong currents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Father of two jailed for shoplifting
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A father of two was jailed for one month by the magistrate's court today after stealing items from a supermarket including deodorant, insect repellent, canned sardines, mayonnaise and flour.
Magistrate Faezahnoor Hassan also imposed a RM300 fine on Mohd Rashid Mohd Noh, 44, an e-hailing driver, after he pleaded guilty.
He was ordered to serve an additional seven days in jail if he failed to pay the fine. The magistrate ordered him to begin serving his sentence immediately.
Rashid was charged with stealing 20 items worth RM404.22, including a bottle of hair dye priced at RM46, instant coffee valued at RM38.43 and a bottle of shampoo costing RM26.45, at 8.10pm at a supermarket in Taman Maluri on April 18.
The offence was framed under Section 380 of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum 10 years' jail and a fine upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Nur Farhana Mohamad Poad urged the court to impose a proportionate sentence as a deterrent.
In mitigation, National Legal Aid Foundation lawyer M. Soosai Raj sought a minimum jail term, saying the accused earned RM2,500 a month and supported his two school-going children.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia faces shortage of over 5,900 veterinarians
Article:
CYBERJAYA: Malaysia is facing a shortage of more than 5,900 veterinary professionals, with only about 2,600 active practitioners currently serving the country, says Veterinary Services Department director-general Dr Mohd Noor Hisham Mohd Haron.
He said the gap had become increasingly pressing amid rising demand for veterinary services, driven by higher pet ownership, the expansion of the livestock industry, and stricter export certification and international compliance requirements.
"Compared with international benchmarks, the country actually needs at least 8,500 veterinarians to meet current demand. This means we are still facing a shortage of more than 5,900 veterinary professionals.
"Our current ratio also shows that we have only about one veterinarian for every 13,700 people, whereas international benchmarks recommend one for every 5,000.
"These statistics send a clear signal that the country's needs far exceed existing capacity," he said in his opening speech at the Veterinary Day celebration here today.
Dr Mohd Noor Hisham said demand for veterinary services continued to grow rapidly, adding that more than half of Malaysian households now owned pets, while the country has only about 650 registered veterinary clinics.
He said that the livestock sector required comprehensive veterinary oversight across all stages of production to ensure sustainable development, efficiency, and compliance with international standards.
"The export industry also depends on veterinary certification and compliance with international standards to penetrate global markets. This means demand is growing, but current capacity remains limited," he added.
Dr Mohd Noor Hisham also highlighted challenges in attracting talent to the public sector, adding that many veterinary graduates were opting for private practice due to better pay and more attractive career incentives.
"This issue must be addressed through a more strategic approach to ensure the public sector remains competitive and relevant," he said.
He stressed that veterinary talent development must remain a priority to meet the country's future needs.
He said the two main public institutions offering Doctor of Veterinary Medicine programmes – Universiti Putra Malaysia and Universiti Malaysia Kelantan – produced only about 180 to 220 graduates annually, despite receiving thousands of applications each year.
"This is still insufficient to meet the country's expanding needs," he said, welcoming the introduction of the Veterinary Medicine programme by Management and Science University in August last year.
"Several other institutions have also shown interest in this field. This is a very positive step and should continue to be supported," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Airod, Airbus partner to strengthen maintenance for RMAF's A400M fleet
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Aircraft maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO) provider Airod Sdn Bhd has signed a memorandum of understanding with Airbus Defence and Space to strengthen maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO) capabilities for the Royal Malaysian Air Force's (RMAF) Airbus A400M fleet.
The agreement, signed at the Defence Services Asia 2026 (DSA 2026) exhibition today, will see both parties collaborate to enhance technical expertise, standards and best practices in maintaining the RMAF's four A400M military transport aircraft.
The signing was witnessed by Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin.
National Aerospace and Defence Industries Sdn Bhd group president Datuk Edron Hayata Ahmad said the partnership marked a key step in strengthening Malaysia's defence aerospace ecosystem.
"This partnership with Airbus Defence and Space marks an important step in strengthening Malaysia's defence aerospace ecosystem.
"By advancing local MRO capabilities for the A400M platform, we are supporting the operational readiness of the RMAF while positioning Malaysia as a credible regional hub for military aircraft maintenance," he said.
He added that Airod remained committed to driving technology transfer, developing skilled local talent and supporting the nation's industrial collaboration agenda to elevate Malaysia's aerospace industry to global standards.
As the original equipment manufacturer of the A400M, Airbus Defence and Space will support capability development through the transfer of technical knowledge, providing a structured pathway to enhance in-country MRO capabilities.
Under the collaboration, both parties will explore the development of A400M technical training programmes, out-of-scheme structural repairs and the expansion of specialised MRO services, including advanced non-destructive testing and aircraft painting.
Airod is also expected to be positioned as a qualified local industrial partner and an Airbus-approved supplier, supporting the long-term sustainment of the RMAF's A400M fleet.
As part of the initiative, Airod will collaborate with Satys Aerospace, a Tier One Airbus partner, on aircraft painting activities.
This is expected to introduce advanced painting technologies, materials and global best practices, further strengthening local technical capabilities in line with international aerospace standards.
The collaboration reflects Malaysia's broader push to strengthen its defence industrial base through technology transfer, local capability development and greater self-reliance, in line with frameworks such as the Industrial Collaboration Programme and the National Defence Industry Policy.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Terengganu divorce cases hit seven-year high, assemblyman warns of social crisis
Article:
KUALA TERENGGANU: A sharp rise in divorce cases in Terengganu is an early warning of a social crisis that requires urgent and comprehensive action, said Ladang assemblyman Zuraida Mohd Noor.
She said the state recorded 3,810 divorce cases in 2025, a significant increase from 2,798 in 2024 and 1,802 in 2023, marking the highest figures in seven years.
Data from the Syariah Judiciary Department also placed Terengganu among the eight states with the highest number of cases in the first nine months of last year.
"This is not just statistics, but a warning of a social crisis. Most cases involve young couples married for less than 10 years, driven by economic pressure, poor communication and emotional instability," she said during the debate on the Royal Address at the state assembly sitting at Wisma Darul Iman today.
Zuraida proposed four strategic measures, including strengthening economic stability through job and entrepreneurship opportunities and making financial management education compulsory before marriage.
She also called for improved pre-marriage courses with practical modules, addressing third-party interference, and strengthening intervention on social issues, particularly drug addiction.
However, she stressed that divorce is not necessarily a failure but a last resort when a marriage can no longer be sustained.
Zuraida noted that 296 domestic violence cases were reported in Terengganu in 2025, with conflict and drug influence cited as primary causes.
On cancer among women, she said cases rose by 40 per cent over five years, from 1,464 in 2021 to 2,004 in 2025, based on Sultanah Nur Zahirah Hospital data.
"Common cancers include breast, colorectal, uterine, leukaemia and ovarian. Obesity is a key contributing factor, in line with findings from the National Health and Morbidity Survey.
"The situation is worrying, with 76 per cent of the population classified as obese, while one in three teenagers faces weight issues," she said.
She urged for stricter school nutrition standards, practical healthy eating education, and wider awareness of the link between obesity and cancer risks. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: All poultry farms in Perak to adopt closed-house system by 2027
Article:
IPOH: The state government has set a deadline until the end of this year for all poultry farmers to transition to closed-house systems, with full enforcement to begin on Jan 1, 2027.
State Rural Development, Plantation, Agriculture and Food Industry Committee chairman Datuk Mohd Zolkafly Harun said the move is aimed at accelerating the industry's shift towards a more modern, sustainable and environmentally friendly model.
Zolkafly said the transition would also help address long-standing environmental concerns, including fly infestations and foul odours commonly linked to open-house poultry farms.
"The state government has taken note of public concerns.
"In addition to this policy, a state-level task force has been established involving multiple agencies, elected representatives, farmers and local communities to ensure the issues are addressed comprehensively," he said.
He said this during his winding-up speech at the State Legislative Assembly sitting in Bangunan Perak Darul Ridzuan here, today.
Zolkafly said the poultry farming sector in Perak is already undergoing transformation, with 203 farms having fully adopted the closed-house system.
He said 177 farms are still operating using open-house systems, including 52 owned by licensed and active Bumiputera entrepreneurs statewide.
"The shift to closed-house systems not only enhances biosecurity, but also improves production efficiency and strengthens the industry's resilience," he said.
In a related development, the Lintang assemblyman said the food crop subsector is facing mounting pressure due to rising input costs driven by global uncertainties, including conflicts in the Middle East.
He said costs for key inputs such as seeds, fertilisers, pesticides and diesel, as well as mechanisation and logistics, have increased, while farm operation costs, including ploughing and land levelling, have surged by between 70 and 80 per cent.
As such, he said the government has stepped up assistance for padi farmers, including increasing the Agri Commodity Assistance from RM300 to RM400, and the ploughing incentive from RM160 to RM300 per hectare per season.
"A total of 15,460 padi farmers have directly benefited, involving 9,899 farmers under the Kerian Integrated Agricultural Development Area (IADA) and 5,561 under Seberang Perak IADA," he said.
He added that under the 13th Malaysia Plan, RM19.84 million has been allocated for Kerian IADA, including RM18.44 million for padi development and management programmes, while RM12.002 million has been channelled to Seberang Perak IADA.
"The allocation covers soil conservation, liming, land levelling, as well as upgrading drainage systems and farm infrastructure to reduce operating costs and improve water management efficiency," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Drug-laced vape liquids containing furanyl fentanyl seized in KL
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police recently uncovered the sale of drug-laced liquids containing furanyl fentanyl following raids during a joint operation in Sentul and Cheras.
The synthetic opioid, a derivative of fentanyl, is extremely potent and poses a high risk of overdose even in small quantities.
Federal Narcotics Crime Investigation Department (NCID) director Datuk Hussein Omar Khan said police seized nine bottles of the liquid drug weighing 16.86kg during a raid held under Op Vape 1.0 on April 13.
"The discovery was made during Op Vape 1.0, conducted from April 13 to 17, which involved the police's Narcotics Crime Investigation Department working alongside several enforcement agencies.
"The joint operation targeted premises suspected of selling vape products laced with drugs, leading to widespread inspections and multiple seizures nationwide," he said.
Hussein said investigations showed the liquid was used to lace vape cartridges with the highly dangerous drug.
"We arrested a man and woman aged 23 and 31 during the raid," he said.
Hussein said a separate raid on April 17 in Taman Mastiara, Sentul, led the police to the arrests of two people aged 22 and 25.
"Upon further inspections,  we found and seized 155 vape cartridges containing 1.7kg of suspected furanyl fentanyl and 22 packets of MDMA powder weighing 0.66kg.
"Based on our intelligence, the drug-laced vapes were not sold openly, but were only sold to certain buyers," he said.
Hussein said furanyl fentanyl was between 50 and 100 times more potent than heroin and morphine.
The drug can cause unexpected overdoses where symptoms include rapid onset of respiratory failure, pinpoint pupils, and loss of consciousness.
Last week, police foiled an attempt to introduce fentanyl, the deadly synthetic drug often dubbed the
"zombie drug"
, into Malaysia after it was found concealed in vape cartridges during coordinated raids in the Klang Valley.
The 400 vape cartridges were among more than half a tonne of drugs worth over RM31.5 million seized after police crippled a syndicate believed to be supplied by a drug ring operating within the Golden Triangle.
The fentanyl discovery has raised concerns that traffickers may be testing the local market for demand.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran hangs man convicted of setting mosque on fire during pre-war protests
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran executed on Tuesday a man convicted of helping set fire to a major mosque in Tehran and of collaborating with Israel and the United States during pre-war protests, the judiciary said.
"Amir Ali Mirjafari... one of the armed elements collaborating with the enemy who had attempted to set fire to the Gholhak Grand Mosque and was the leader of the Mossad network's anti-security activities in that area, was hanged this morning," the judiciary's Mizan Online website reported.
It added that his death sentence, confirmed by the Supreme Court, was based on acting on behalf of "the Zionist regime, the hostile US government, and hostile groups against the security of the country by setting fire to the Gholhak Grand Mosque and public facilities."
Mizan said the incident took place during protests that broke out in Iran in late December over rising living costs before evolving into nationwide anti-government demonstrations that peaked on Jan 8 and 9.
Iran has in recent weeks carried out multiple executions of people linked to the protests, whom authorities accused of acting on behalf of Israel, the United States or opposition groups, including members of the banned People's Mujahedin (MEK).
Iran has been at war with the United States and Israel since Feb 28. A fragile two-week ceasefire has been in place since April 8.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Labuan Customs seizes three containers filled with RM343,400 worth of liquor
Article:
LABUAN: The Labuan Customs Department seized three 20-foot containers of liquor worth RM343,400 after a company allegedly exceeded its import quota.
The seizure, which took place at the port here, involves unpaid duties and taxes amounting to RM948,168.61.
Labuan Customs director Aspalila Ag Tuah said the enforcement operations team acted on intelligence to intercept the shipment.
"The operation, conducted at 10am on March 17, led to the confiscation of containers imported by a company holding a valid import licence.
"Initial investigations revealed the company had an approved licence for the period from March 20, 2025, to March 19, 2026, with an authorised quota of 250,000 litres.
"However, the company exceeded this by importing an additional 52,746.24 litres within the licence period," she told a press conference today.
Aspalila said liquor importation is strictly regulated, requiring adherence to quotas and the use of authorised tax stamps under Regulation 42(1) of the Customs Regulations 2019.
She said the syndicate's modus operandi involved declaring the goods through two Customs Form No. 1 submissions while transporting the liquor in three separate containers.
The case is being investigated under Section 138 of the Customs Act 1967.
Aspalila said the department remains committed to addressing non-compliance and would intensify enforcement to curb smuggling.
She urged the public to assist by reporting smuggling activities involving cigarettes, liquor, firecrackers, drugs, and vehicles.
Information can be shared via the Customs toll-free line at 1-800-88-8855 or at the nearest Customs office. Aspalila assured that the identities of informants would be kept confidential.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysians should be prudent in fuel to stretch energy buffer beyond June
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysians are urged to be prudent in fuel consumption to stretch the country's energy buffer beyond June as global uncertainties are expected to persist, said Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong.
He said the country remains in a relatively comfortable position for now, but early conservation is necessary to ensure supply stability in the months ahead.
"Our supplies are comfortable, at least until June.
"But if possible, we need to be prudent in our usage so that our supply can last longer, possibly into July or August," he said after the Securities Commission-Asean+3 Macroeconomic Research Office (AMRO) Regional Economic Outlook Seminar: asean at a Crossroads here today.
Liew said the evolving global environment requires Malaysia not only to manage near-term resources but also to strengthen its economic fundamentals, particularly in addressing vulnerabilities within supply chains.
He said there was a need to identify gaps in critical sectors and close them through more innovative and coordinated approaches to enhance resilience.
At the same time, Liew said the shifting global order towards a more multipolar landscape underscores the importance of regional cooperation, especially through asean, to strengthen collective influence.
"Malaysia is not a big country. It is important for us to operate within Asean so that we can speak with a stronger, collective voice," he said.
Liew said current challenges should be viewed as an opportunity to build stronger economic resilience, including through energy transition efforts and deeper regional integration.
Looking back on Malaysia's experience, the deputy minister cited the establishment of Petronas as a key example of how the country had successfully navigated past crises.
He said the national oil company was formed in response to the 1974 global oil crisis, a move that has since strengthened Malaysia's energy position and contributed significantly to its economic development.
"Today, we are in a more comfortable position in terms of oil, and that is something we should not take for granted," he said, adding that the country owes much to the strategic decisions made during that period.
He said the experience demonstrates how crises can be turned into long-term advantages through timely and strategic policy responses.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Belgium slams Israel's Lebanon actions as 'totally unacceptable'
Article:
LUXEMBOURG: Israel's actions in Lebanon are "totally unacceptable", Belgian Foreign Minister Maxime Prevot said on Tuesday ahead of a meeting with EU foreign ministers in Luxembourg.
"Israel's conduct is completely unacceptable. Of course, we must firmly condemn Hizbollah's initial attacks, which, in seeking to show solidarity with Iran, dragged Lebanon into a war it did not want, as well as Israel's disproportionate and indiscriminate response."
He also said Belgium is calling for at least a partial suspension of the EU's Association's Agreement with Israel, adding that Belgium is "aware that a full suspension is probably out of reach given the positions of the various European countries."--REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Drink-driving: Woman jailed, fined RM13,000 over Genting Klang crash
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 42-year-old woman was jailed for one day and fined RM13,000 after pleading guilty to drink-driving following a crash involving a motorcycle at Jalan Genting Klang on Monday.
The accident occurred at 8.22pm at a traffic light junction near the Road Transport Department (JPJ) in Wangsa Maju, involving a white BMW X1 sDrive20i and a red Yamaha LC135 motorcycle.
Kuala Lumpur Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department chief Mohd Zamzuri Mohd Isa said a report was lodged at 10.15pm the same night, while a video of the incident surfaced on social media the following morning.
Initial investigations found the woman had failed to stop at a red light before colliding with the motorcycle, which was travelling straight on a green light.
